facts.text: Astoria began as Hallet's Cove, a 17th‑century settlement founded by William Hallet in 1652, before becoming the namesake of John Jacob Astor.
The neighborhood’s historic core includes Bohemian Hall, the oldest beer garden in New York City, founded in 1910 by Irish, Italian, Czech, and Slovak immigrants.
Steinway & Sons relocated to Astoria in the 1870s, creating Steinway Village—a model company town complete with worker housing, a church, and a streetcar line to the river.
Socrates Sculpture Park, founded in 1986 on a reclaimed landfill, has grown into a world‑class open‑air art space that reflects Astoria’s creative resilience.
Astoria’s waterfront park, featuring an Olympic‑size pool designed by Aymar Embury II in 1936, hosted Olympic trials, concerts, and countless summer afternoons.
The Museum of the Moving Image in Astoria showcases the neighborhood’s film‑history legacy, celebrating its role as a backdrop for movies, TV shows, and streaming series.
